bytes.topic: Project Setup & Config — zero-config vs explicit config, config file structure
bytes.title: webpack Config — Zero-Config Defaults vs. Explicit webpack.config.js
bytes.body:
webpack 4+ ships with production/development mode defaults that eliminate boilerplate for simple projects: run `webpack --mode production` and you get Terser minification, tree-shaking via `optimization.usedExports`, and deterministic chunk IDs — no config file needed. But the moment you need custom loaders, multiple entry points, or output path control, you need `webpack.config.js`.

The config file exports a plain object (or a function for env-aware configs). `entry` defaults to `./src/index.js`; `output.path` defaults to `./dist`. Override only what you must — webpack merges your explicit keys with the mode defaults, not replaces them entirely.

For team projects, prefer the functional form: `module.exports = (env, argv) => ({})`. `argv.mode` gives you `'production'` or `'development'` so you can conditionally enable `devtool: 'source-map'` without separate config files. Use `webpack-merge` to split base/dev/prod configs only when differences grow beyond 20–30 lines — premature splitting adds navigation overhead with no real benefit.

bytes.topic: Dev Server — HMR, proxy setup, HTTPS in dev
bytes.title: webpack Dev Server — HMR, Proxy Setup, and HTTPS in Dev
bytes.body: webpack-dev-server's `devServer` config block controls three critical dev-time behaviors. HMR (Hot Module Replacement) is enabled by default in webpack 5 when `hot: true`; it patches modules in-place via `webpack/hot/dev-server` without a full page reload, preserving component state. For API proxying, `devServer.proxy` delegates matching routes to your backend — essential when your frontend runs on port 3000 but your API lives on 5000. The `pathRewrite` option strips prefixes before forwarding. For HTTPS, `devServer.server: 'https'` spins up a local TLS server using webpack-dev-server's self-signed cert; swap in your own via `server: { type: 'https', options: { key, cert } }`. Combine this with `allowedHosts: 'all'` cautiously — it disables the host check that prevents DNS rebinding attacks. In webpack 5, `devServer.client.webSocketURL` must match your HTTPS scheme so the HMR websocket doesn't get blocked by mixed-content policies.

bytes.topic: Code Splitting — manual chunks, dynamic imports, route-based splitting
bytes.title: webpack Code Splitting — Manual Chunks, Dynamic Imports, and Route-Based Splitting
bytes.body:
webpack splits code through three complementary mechanisms. Dynamic imports (`import()`) are the foundation — webpack treats every `import()` call as a split point and emits a separate chunk. For route-based splitting, wrap each route component in a dynamic import; webpack resolves the dependency graph at build time and defers loading until the route activates.

Manual chunks via `optimization.splitChunks` give you surgical control. The `cacheGroups` API lets you extract vendor libraries, shared utilities, or any module subset into named chunks. Setting `chunks: 'all'` enables splitting across both async and initial chunks — critical for extracting large deps like `lodash` or `react-dom` that would otherwise bloat your entry bundle.

Magic comments let you name async chunks and control loading behavior: `/* webpackChunkName: 'dashboard' */` sets the output filename, while `/* webpackPrefetch: true */` instructs the browser to prefetch the chunk during idle time using a `<link rel='prefetch'>` tag injected by webpack's runtime.

bytes.topic: Tree Shaking — how it works, why side effects matter, marking packages as pure
bytes.title: webpack Tree Shaking — How It Works and Why sideEffects Matters
bytes.body:
webpack's tree shaking relies on ES module static analysis. Because `import`/`export` bindings are resolved at parse time — not runtime — webpack can build a precise dependency graph and mark unused exports as dead code for Terser to eliminate.

The critical lever is the `sideEffects` field in `package.json`. Without it, webpack assumes every module has side effects (think polyfills that mutate globals) and won't drop unused files. Set `"sideEffects": false` to tell webpack the entire package is pure. For packages with some side-effectful files, use an array: `"sideEffects": ["*.css", "./src/polyfill.js"]`.

Your own source needs the same treatment. In `webpack.config.js`, set `optimization.sideEffects: true` (default in production mode) to respect `package.json` hints. Combine with `optimization.usedExports: true` so webpack annotates unused exports with `/* unused harmony export */` before Terser strips them.

Common pitfall: CommonJS (`require()`) breaks tree shaking entirely — webpack can't statically analyze dynamic requires. Ensure your libraries ship ESM via the `module` field in `package.json`.

bytes.topic: Bundle Analysis — visualizing size, finding bloat, setting size budgets in CI
bytes.title: webpack Bundle Analysis — Visualizing Size, Finding Bloat, and Enforcing CI Budgets
bytes.body:
webpack's stats JSON is your starting point. Run `webpack --json > stats.json` and feed it into `webpack-bundle-analyzer` to get an interactive treemap of every module by parsed and gzipped size. Spot immediately when a lodash import pulls in 70 kB because you forgot `babel-plugin-lodash` or a dynamic `import()` boundary got swallowed into the main chunk.

For deeper audits, `--profile` flags each module's build time alongside its size — useful when a single dependency balloons after a version bump.

To enforce limits in CI, use `performance` in your webpack config. Set `hints: 'error'` so the build fails on violations instead of just warning. Pair `maxEntrypointSize` and `maxAssetSize` (in bytes) to gate merges on bundle discipline.

For per-chunk granularity, `SpeedMeasurePlugin` combined with a custom `BundleAnalyzerPlugin` in `server` mode lets you diff bundle reports between branches — run `webpack-bundle-analyzer stats.json` in headless mode and compare the generated HTML artifacts in your CI artifacts tab.

bytes.topic: Asset Handling — images, fonts, SVGs, hashing for cache busting
bytes.title: webpack Asset Handling — Images, Fonts, SVGs, and Content Hashing
bytes.body:
webpack 5 replaces `file-loader` and `url-loader` with built-in Asset Modules. Four types cover every static asset need: `asset/resource` emits a separate file and returns its URL; `asset/inline` base64-encodes the asset into the bundle; `asset/source` injects raw content as a string; `asset` auto-picks between resource and inline based on a configurable size threshold (default 8kb).

For SVGs, set type `asset/source` to get the raw markup — enabling inline SVG injection and CSS fill manipulation at runtime. For fonts, `asset/resource` with a generator filename keeps them in a dedicated output directory.

Content hashing is controlled via `output.filename` and `generator.filename` using the `[contenthash]` substitution. webpack recomputes this hash only when the file's content changes, so unchanged assets retain their hash across builds — maximizing CDN cache hits while busting stale files automatically. Use `[contenthash:8]` for shorter hashes in filenames.

bytes.topic: Environment Variables — .env files, build-time vs runtime, define plugin
bytes.title: webpack Environment Variables — DefinePlugin, dotenv, and Build-Time vs Runtime
bytes.body:
webpack has no native `.env` support — you wire it up yourself. The standard approach combines `dotenv` with `DefinePlugin`: parse your `.env` file in `webpack.config.js`, then use `DefinePlugin` to statically replace identifiers at compile time. Every `process.env.API_URL` reference gets inlined as a string literal in the bundle — it's a text substitution, not a real Node.js environment lookup. That distinction matters: values are baked in at build time, so shipping one bundle to multiple environments (staging/prod) requires separate builds or a runtime injection strategy.

For runtime config, skip `DefinePlugin` entirely. Serve a `config.js` from your CDN or server that sets `window.__ENV__`, load it via a `<script>` tag before your bundle, and read from `window.__ENV__` in app code. This lets ops teams change URLs without rebuilding.

Never pass secrets through `DefinePlugin` — anything inlined becomes readable in the bundle. Use it only for public, environment-specific constants like API base URLs or feature flags.

bytes.topic: Source Maps — types, trade-offs in production
bytes.title: webpack Source Maps — Picking the Right devtool for Production
bytes.body:
webpack's `devtool` option controls source map generation, and the wrong choice in production either tanks performance or leaks your source code. There are 20+ variants, but production decisions usually come down to three.

`source-map` generates a separate `.map` file linked via `//# sourceMappingURL`. Full fidelity, safest for production — restrict access at the CDN/server level so end users can't fetch the map. Build time is slowest.

`hidden-source-map` generates the same file but omits the `sourceMappingURL` comment. Perfect for error monitoring tools like Sentry that upload maps directly via their webpack plugin — users never see the link, but your error tracker can symbolicate stack traces.

`nosources-source-map` includes line/column mappings but strips original source content from the map file. Safe to expose publicly — gives you usable stack traces in DevTools without exposing proprietary code.

Avoid `eval-*` variants in production entirely — they use `eval()`, break CSP, and inflate bundle size. Set `devtool` per environment using webpack's `--env` flag or separate config files, never hardcode a single value.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — transpile-only vs type-check, speed vs safety trade-off
bytes.title: webpack + TypeScript — Transpile-Only vs Type-Check: Pick Your Trade-off
bytes.body:
webpack doesn't type-check TypeScript by default — it just transpiles. ts-loader runs tsc under the hood and blocks your build on type errors, giving you safety but costing seconds on every rebuild. babel-loader with @babel/preset-typescript strips types at parse time, no tsc invoked, which makes it 2–5x faster in large projects. The catch: Babel silently ignores type errors entirely.

The practical webpack pattern: use babel-loader in development for fast HMR, and run tsc --noEmit as a parallel npm script or in a ForkTsCheckerWebpackPlugin worker so type errors surface without blocking the main bundle graph. ForkTsCheckerWebpackPlugin spawns a separate process and reports errors as webpack overlay diagnostics — you get near-babel speed with incremental type checking.

In CI, swap to ts-loader with transpileOnly: false to enforce full type safety before merge. Never ship a webpack config that skips type-checking entirely in CI — transpile-only is a dev ergonomics optimization, not a production default.

bytes.topic: CSS Processing — PostCSS, CSS Modules, preprocessor support
bytes.title: webpack CSS Pipeline — PostCSS, CSS Modules, and Preprocessors in One Loader Chain
bytes.body:
webpack processes CSS through a right-to-left loader chain. For Sass + CSS Modules + PostCSS, you need four loaders stacked in the correct order: `style-loader` (or `MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader` for production) → `css-loader` → `postcss-loader` → `sass-loader`. Each transforms the output before passing it upstream.

`css-loader` with `modules: true` enables CSS Modules — it scopes class names by hashing them and exposes a mapping object importable in JS. Set `localIdentName: '[name]__[local]--[hash:base64:5]'` during development for readable names.

`postcss-loader` reads `postcss.config.js` and applies plugins like `autoprefixer` or `cssnano`. Critically, it runs *after* Sass compilation, so it receives plain CSS — not SCSS. This ordering matters: PostCSS cannot parse SCSS syntax.

For production, swap `style-loader` for `MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader` to emit a separate `.css` file rather than injecting styles via JS bundles. Pair it with `optimization.splitChunks` to deduplicate shared CSS across entry points. Use `css-loader`'s `importLoaders: 2` option to ensure PostCSS and Sass re-process `@import`ed files correctly.

bytes.topic: Aliasing & Path Resolution — @ aliases, module resolution config
bytes.title: webpack Path Aliases — Clean Imports with resolve.alias
bytes.body:
Deeply nested relative imports like `../../../components/Button` are fragile and hard to refactor. webpack's `resolve.alias` lets you map shorthand tokens to absolute paths, eliminating traversal chains entirely.

In `webpack.config.js`, set `resolve.alias` to an object where each key is the alias token and the value is an absolute path — typically built with `path.resolve(__dirname, 'src/...')`. webpack rewrites matching import prefixes at bundle time, so `@components/Button` resolves directly to `src/components/Button`.

For TypeScript projects, mirror every alias in `tsconfig.json` under `compilerOptions.paths` — webpack and `tsc` resolve modules independently, so both configs must stay in sync. Use `tsconfig-paths-webpack-plugin` if you'd rather maintain a single source of truth in `tsconfig.json` and have webpack read paths from there automatically.

Aliases also work with `resolve.modules` for global lookup directories, but `resolve.alias` is more explicit and predictable. Prefer exact aliases over directory-level module roots to avoid ambiguous resolutions across loaders.

bytes.topic: Performance — build speed, caching, parallel processing, incremental builds
bytes.title: webpack Persistent Caching — Cutting Rebuild Times by 90%
bytes.body:
webpack 5's filesystem cache (`cache.type: 'filesystem'`) serializes the module graph, chunk graph, and code generation results to disk between builds. On a cold run, webpack processes every module. On subsequent runs, it only reprocesses modules whose content hash changed — skipping the resolve, parse, and transform pipeline for everything else. The cache lives in `node_modules/.cache/webpack` by default and is keyed by a `buildDependencies.config` hash, so it automatically invalidates when your `webpack.config.js` changes.

Pair this with `cache.version` to namespace caches across branches, preventing cross-contamination. For parallelism, `thread-loader` offloads expensive loaders (babel-loader, ts-loader) to a worker pool — place it immediately before the target loader. Use `thread-loader`'s `warmup()` in your config to pre-spawn workers before the build starts, eliminating the thread startup penalty on the first run. Together, filesystem caching + `thread-loader` targeting your heaviest loaders consistently brings incremental rebuilds from 30s+ to under 3s on mid-size TypeScript monorepos.

bytes.topic: Production Optimization — minification, compression (gzip/brotli), dead code elimination
bytes.title: webpack Production Optimization — Minification, Compression, and Tree Shaking
bytes.body:
webpack's production mode (`mode: 'production'`) enables TerserPlugin for JS minification by default, but you can tune it. Set `compress.drop_console: true` to strip console calls and `mangle.toplevel: true` for aggressive identifier renaming. For CSS, pair `css-minimizer-webpack-plugin` with `MiniCssExtractPlugin` — the default `cssnano` preset handles vendor prefix removal and rule merging.

Tree shaking requires ES modules — CommonJS `require()` blocks it entirely. Mark your package as side-effect-free with `"sideEffects": false` in `package.json`, or enumerate affected files (e.g. global CSS imports) so webpack's `usedExports` and `providedExports` analyses can prune safely.

For compression, `CompressionPlugin` generates `.gz` and `.br` static assets at build time — your CDN or nginx serves the pre-compressed file directly, bypassing runtime encoding. Brotli (`algorithm: 'brotliCompress'`) achieves 15–25% smaller output than gzip on text assets. Set `threshold: 10240` to skip compressing files under 10 KB where overhead exceeds savings.

bytes.topic: Plugin System — how plugins extend the build pipeline
bytes.title: webpack Plugin System — Hooking Into the Compiler Lifecycle
bytes.body:
webpack's plugin system is built on Tapable, a hook library that exposes the internal compiler and compilation objects at every stage of the build. Plugins tap into these hooks to read or mutate build state.

The two core objects are `compiler` (the long-lived webpack instance) and `compilation` (a single build run). Each exposes typed hooks — `SyncHook`, `AsyncSeriesHook`, `SyncBailHook`, etc. — that fire at defined lifecycle points: `compiler.hooks.run`, `compilation.hooks.optimizeChunkModules`, `compilation.hooks.processAssets`, and dozens more.

A plugin is just an object with an `apply(compiler)` method. Inside, you tap hooks with `.tap()` (sync), `.tapAsync()` (callback), or `.tapPromise()` (Promise). The `processAssets` hook, for example, lets you inject, replace, or remove emitted files before they hit disk — useful for inlining manifests, rewriting hashes, or generating reports.

Hook execution order is deterministic: taps fire in registration order within a stage, and `AsyncSeriesHook` chains them sequentially, ensuring each plugin's async work completes before the next runs.

bytes.topic: Vite-specific — native ESM in dev, esbuild pre-bundling, Rollup in prod
bytes.title: webpack vs Vite's Dev Pipeline: Native ESM, esbuild Pre-Bundling, and Rollup Explained for webpack Users
bytes.body:
webpack bundles the entire module graph before the browser sees a single byte — even in dev. Every `import` is resolved, transformed, and stitched into a bundle upfront via its JavaScript-based compiler. Vite inverts this: in dev mode it serves raw ES modules directly over HTTP, letting the browser do import resolution on demand. `node_modules` are pre-bundled once by esbuild (written in Go, 10–100× faster than webpack's JS pipeline), then cached. Only request-triggered modules are processed per save.

In production, Vite swaps esbuild for Rollup because Rollup's tree-shaking and code-splitting output is more mature. webpack's production mode achieves comparable results via `optimization.usedExports: true`, `optimization.sideEffects: true`, and `TerserPlugin`.

The critical caveat for webpack users evaluating Vite: Vite's dev and prod pipelines run on two different bundlers. Module resolution edge cases that pass in dev can silently fail in production. webpack's unified pipeline — one bundler, one config, both modes — eliminates this class of parity bug. webpack 5's `experiments.lazyCompilation` narrows the cold-start gap without sacrificing that consistency.

bytes.topic: Webpack-specific — loaders vs plugins, module federation
bytes.title: webpack Loaders vs Plugins — And Where Module Federation Fits
bytes.body:
Loaders and plugins operate at different pipeline stages. Loaders transform individual module files before they enter the dependency graph — `babel-loader` transpiles JS, `css-loader` resolves `@import`, `raw-loader` inlines assets as strings. They run per-file, chained right-to-left in the `use` array. Plugins hook into webpack's compiler lifecycle via Tapable events (`emit`, `compilation`, `afterOptimize`) and operate on the entire bundle graph — `MiniCssExtractPlugin` pulls CSS out of JS chunks, `BundleAnalyzerPlugin` instruments the `done` hook.

Module Federation sits at the plugin level but redefines the module resolution contract entirely. `ModuleFederationPlugin` exposes and consumes remote chunks at runtime, not build time. The host app's webpack runtime fetches remote entry manifests, resolves shared singletons (e.g. a single React instance across microfrontends), and loads federated modules via async dynamic `import()`. Critically, shared dependencies declared in `shared: { react: { singleton: true } }` are deduplicated across remotes — without this, each remote bundles its own React, breaking hook state.

bytes.topic: Monorepo Support — workspace packages, shared configs, turborepo integration
bytes.title: webpack Monorepo Support — Workspace Packages, Shared Configs, and Turborepo Integration
bytes.body:
webpack doesn't natively understand workspace packages, but it resolves them correctly when you configure `resolve.alias` or rely on Node's module resolution with symlinks. Set `resolve.symlinks: true` (default) so webpack follows pnpm/yarn workspace symlinks to their real disk locations — critical for source maps and HMR to work correctly across packages.

For shared webpack configs in a monorepo, use `webpack-merge` to compose a base config from a root `packages/webpack-config` package, then extend per-app. Each app's `webpack.config.js` imports and merges the shared preset.

When layering Turborepo on top, Turborepo's `pipeline` caches webpack build outputs (the `dist/` directory) keyed by input hashes. Define `outputs: ["dist/**"]` in `turbo.json` so Turborepo knows what to restore. Turborepo doesn't replace webpack — it caches its artifacts. For type-only packages consumed by webpack's TypeScript loader (`ts-loader` or `babel-loader`), add them to `dependsOn` in the pipeline so they build before webpack runs.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — config sharing, extending base configs, CI build caching
bytes.title: webpack Config Sharing, Base Extension, and CI Filesystem Caching
bytes.body:
webpack config sharing typically relies on `webpack-merge`. Split your setup into `webpack.common.js`, `webpack.dev.js`, and `webpack.prod.js`. Each environment file calls `merge(common, envSpecific)` — layering in `devServer`, `devtool`, or `TerserPlugin` without duplicating entry points or `resolve.alias` definitions.

For CI build caching, webpack 5's built-in `cache` option with `type: 'filesystem'` serializes the module graph to disk between runs. Set `cache.buildDependencies.config` pointing to all config files — webpack auto-invalidates the cache when any of them change. On GitHub Actions, cache the `.cache/webpack` directory keyed on a hash of `package-lock.json` plus your config files. A warmed cache can cut incremental CI builds from 3–4 minutes to under 30 seconds on mid-sized apps.

One sharp edge: `cache.name` defaults to the webpack `mode`, so dev and prod caches won't collide. But if you run multiple webpack configs in the same repo — a library build alongside an app build — set explicit `cache.name` values per config to prevent cross-contamination of the serialized module graph.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — build time tracking, bundle size regression alerts in CI
bytes.title: webpack Build Monitoring — Track Build Times and Catch Bundle Regressions in CI
bytes.body:
webpack's `StatsPlugin` and the built-in `stats` object give you precise per-asset, per-chunk timing data. Pipe `stats.toJson({ timings: true, assets: true })` to a JSON file after every build, then diff it in CI to catch regressions before they merge.

For build time tracking, wrap `webpack()` in a Node script and read `stats.endTime - stats.startTime`. For bundle size gates, use `webpack-bundle-analyzer`'s `statsFilename` output or the lighter `bundlesize` package, which reads your `stats.json` and enforces per-asset byte limits defined in `package.json`.

In GitHub Actions, store the `stats.json` as a build artifact and compare it against the baseline from `main` using a custom script or the `relative-ci/agent` action, which posts a PR comment with a size diff table. Set `performance.hints: 'error'` in your webpack config to fail the build outright when any asset breaches `performance.maxAssetSize` or `performance.maxEntrypointSize` thresholds — no external tooling required for hard limits.
